turns.content: thinking about corn today. it's still cold but planting season is coming and I need to get organized
It's good to plan ahead while the ground is still resting. What kind of corn are you looking to plant this year?

traditional varieties. diné corn. we have blue corn, white corn, yellow, and a multicolored one my aunt calls naadą́ą́' łitsooí though I probably spelled that wrong. she's been saving seeds from her plants for as long as I can remember. you know, these aren't the tall straight rows you see in Iowa. our corn is different. shorter, tougher. built for this land

That's a really important distinction — landrace varieties that have adapted over generations to arid conditions, sandy soil, and wide temperature swings. They may not look like commercial corn but they're far more resilient. Are you planting from your aunt's saved seeds, or do you have your own stock too?

my aunt gives me seeds every year. she is the keeper. she's got jars and jars going back decades. she says every seed carries memory of every harvest before it. I believe her. these seeds know this soil better than any hybrid from a catalog

Your aunt is a seed keeper in the truest sense — that's a role that carries a lot of responsibility and knowledge. Seeds that have been selected and saved through decades of local growing conditions are genuinely adapted in ways that commercial varieties can't match. What's your planting setup like? How much space are you working with?

small plot behind the house. nothing big. enough to feed the family and have some to dry for winter. we plant in hills not rows. you know the old way. corn, squash, and beans together. the three sisters. the corn gives the beans something to climb, the beans fix nitrogen in the soil, and the squash covers the ground to keep moisture in. it all works together

The three sisters method is one of the most elegant companion planting systems ever developed — centuries of agricultural knowledge in one planting hill. Each plant giving something the others need. Do you follow a specific timing for when you put each one in, or do they all go in together?

corn goes in first. it needs a head start to get tall enough for the beans. then beans and squash a couple weeks later. and the timing isn't by calendar exactly. my grandmother planted when the cottonwood leaves were the size of a squirrel's ear. I still use that. when those little leaves unfurl that's when the soil is warm enough

Squirrel's ear — that's a perfect natural indicator. Cottonwood leafing out corresponds to soil temperatures reaching about 50-60 degrees, which is exactly the germination range for corn. Your grandmother was reading the same data a thermometer would give her, just through the landscape. Do you save any of your own seed from the harvest, or does your aunt handle all the seed keeping?

I've started saving some of my own the last few years. pick the best ears, let them dry completely, store them in a cool dark place. my aunt showed me how to check for good kernels. you press your thumbnail into one and if it dents but doesn't crack it's ready. if it cracks it's too dry. if it's soft it's not dry enough

That thumbnail test is brilliant — it's a quick moisture check that tells you everything you need to know. Too dry and you lose viability, too moist and you get mold in storage. Are you seeing good germination rates from your saved seed, or is it still early in your seed-saving practice?

pretty good so far. not as good as my aunt's because she has been doing this for 40 years and she has an eye for it. but I'm learning. you know it's funny, I can read a lab panel in two seconds but seed selection is a whole different kind of knowledge. humbling

That's a great observation — clinical expertise and agricultural knowledge are both built on pattern recognition and experience, but they come from completely different traditions. And seed saving is forgiving enough that you'll get better each year just by doing it. The blue corn — is that what you use for blue cornmeal?

yes. blue corn ground on the metate for blue cornmeal mush, for bread, for everything. the color deepens when you cook it. there's nothing like it from a store. Raymond says my blue corn bread is the reason he married me but I think it was the sheep honestly. man knows a good flock when he sees one

Ha — maybe it was the full package. The bread, the sheep, the whole operation. There's something special about growing the corn, grinding it yourself, and feeding your family from it. I hope the cottonwood leaves cooperate this spring and you get a good planting season.
shi éí I'll be watching those trees every day starting in March. ahéhee' for letting me talk about corn. sometimes it's nice to think about growing things instead of fixing things
Growing things is its own kind of healing. I enjoyed hearing about it — your aunt's seeds, the three sisters, all of it. Good luck this spring. I hope the rain cooperates too.
